preamble.summary: Isaiah envisions a king who reigns in righteousness and princes who rule in justice — a portrait of governance as it was always meant to be. Under this reign, human perception is restored: eyes see, ears hear, and the rash gain understanding. The chapter then pivots to a sobering address to complacent women, warning that the land will become desolate until the Spirit is poured out from on high. When that outpouring comes, wilderness becomes garden, justice dwells even in the wild places, and the fruit of righteousness is shalom — peace, quietness, and confident trust forever.
preamble.remarkable: The movement from human kingship to divine Spirit-outpouring is striking. Verses 1-8 describe what a righteous king produces; verses 15-20 describe what only the Spirit of God can produce. The implication is that the ultimate righteous king and the Spirit's outpouring are inseparable realities — a theology that finds its fullest expression in the messianic hope.
preamble.friction: We have rendered tsedaqah consistently as "righteousness" and mishpat as "justice" throughout, preserving the covenantal weight of both terms. The phrase ma'aseh hatstsedaqah (v.17) is rendered "the work of righteousness" rather than "the effect" to maintain the sense of active, produced fruit. Shalvah (v.17) carries a meaning beyond mere "assurance" — it is the quiet confidence of one who trusts covenant promises.
preamble.connections: The Spirit-outpouring of verse 15 anticipates Joel 2:28-29 and finds its Pentecost fulfillment in Acts 2. The wilderness-to-garden transformation echoes Eden theology and connects to Isaiah 35:1-2. The portrait of the righteous king resonates with Isaiah 11:1-5 and Jeremiah 23:5-6.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֥ן לְצֶ֖דֶק יִמְלָךְ־מֶ֑לֶךְ וּלְשָׂרִ֖ים לְמִשְׁפָּ֥ט יָשֹֽׂרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, a king shall reign in righteousness, and princes shall rule in judgment:
verses.rendering:
Behold — a king shall reign in righteousness,
and princes shall govern in justice.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, rightness, justice, what is right
key_terms.note: Here tsedeq describes the moral quality of the king's reign — governance aligned with the covenant standard of God.

translator_notes: The opening hen ("behold") signals prophetic announcement. This is not mere political commentary but messianic vision — the king who will reign as kingship was designed to function.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָיָה־אִ֥ישׁ כְּמַחֲבֵא־ר֖וּחַ וְסֵ֣תֶר זָ֑רֶם כְּפַלְגֵי־מַ֣יִם בְּצָי֔וֹן כְּצֵ֥ל סֶֽלַע־כָּבֵ֖ד בְּאֶ֥רֶץ עֲיֵפָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And a man shall be as an hiding place from the wind, and a covert from the tempest; as rivers of water in a dry place, as the shadow of a great rock in a weary land.
verses.rendering:
And each one shall be like a shelter from the wind,
a refuge from the storm,
like streams of water in a dry place,
like the shadow of a massive rock in a weary land.
verses.expanded_rendering: The imagery is rich with covenant care: machabe-ruach ("shelter from the wind") and seter zarem ("refuge from the storm") portray leaders who protect rather than exploit — the very reversal of what Isaiah has condemned in the rulers of chapters 1-31.
translator_notes: The subject "each one" (ish) likely refers to the princes of verse 1, though some read it as referring to the king himself. Under righteous governance, every leader becomes a source of protection and refreshment.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹ֥א תִשְׁעֶ֖ינָה עֵינֵ֣י רֹאִ֑ים וְאָזְנֵ֥י שֹׁמְעִ֖ים תִּקְשַֽׁבְנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: And the eyes of them that see shall not be dim, and the ears of them that hear shall hearken.
verses.rendering:
And the eyes of those who see shall not be shut,
and the ears of those who hear shall be attentive.
translator_notes: This reverses the judicial blinding of Isaiah 6:9-10. Under the righteous king, the curse of dulled perception is lifted — eyes truly see and ears truly hear.

verses.text_hebrew: וּלְבַ֥ב נִמְהָרִ֖ים יָבִ֣ין לָדָ֑עַת וּלְשׁ֣וֹן עִלְּגִ֔ים תְּמַהֵ֖ר לְדַבֵּ֥ר צָחֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart also of the rash shall understand knowledge, and the tongue of the stammerers shall be ready to speak plainly.
verses.rendering:
The heart of the rash shall understand knowledge,
and the tongue of the stammering shall speak clearly and fluently.
translator_notes: The transformation extends to intellect and speech. Those who once rushed to judgment (nimharim) gain discernment, and those who could not articulate truth find their voice.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יִקָּרֵ֥א ע֛וֹד לְנָבָ֖ל נָדִ֑יב וּלְכִילַ֕י לֹ֥א יֵאָמֵ֖ר שׁ֥וֹעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The vile person shall be no more called liberal, nor the churl said to be bountiful.
verses.rendering:
The fool shall no longer be called noble,
nor the scoundrel be said to be generous.

key_terms.semantic_range: fool, senseless one, morally deficient person
key_terms.note: Naval is not intellectual deficiency but moral bankruptcy — the same word applied to Nabal in 1 Samuel 25:25. Under righteous rule, moral categories are no longer inverted.

translator_notes: In a corrupt society, titles are divorced from character. The righteous king restores moral clarity — things are called what they truly are. This is a vision of cultural truth-telling.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י נָבָל֙ נְבָלָ֣ה יְדַבֵּ֔ר וְלִבּ֖וֹ יַעֲשֶׂה־אָ֑וֶן לַעֲשׂ֣וֹת חֹ֗נֶף וּלְדַבֵּ֤ר אֶל־יְהוָה֙ תּוֹעָ֔ה לְהָרִ֞יק נֶ֤פֶשׁ רָעֵב֙ וּמַשְׁקֶ֣ה צָמֵ֔א יַחְסִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: For the vile person will speak villany, and his heart will work iniquity, to practise hypocrisy, and to utter error against the LORD, to make empty the soul of the hungry, and he will cause the drink of the thirsty to fail.
verses.rendering:
For the fool speaks folly,
and his heart devises wickedness —
to practice godlessness
and to speak error against the LORD,
to leave the soul of the hungry empty
and to deprive the thirsty of drink.
translator_notes: Isaiah defines what makes the naval a naval: his speech, his heart, his religion, and his social ethics are all corrupt. The fool's wickedness is comprehensive — theological error joined to social injustice.

verses.text_hebrew: וְכֵלַ֖י כֵּלָ֣יו רָעִ֑ים ה֚וּא זִמּ֣וֹת יָעָ֔ץ לְחַבֵּ֤ל עֲנָוִים֙ (ענִיִּים) בְּאִמְרֵי־שֶׁ֔קֶר וּבְדַבֵּ֥ר אֶבְי֖וֹן מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: The instruments also of the churl are evil: he deviseth wicked devices to destroy the poor with lying words, even when the needy speaketh right.
verses.rendering:
As for the scoundrel — his schemes are evil;
he devises wicked plans
to ruin the afflicted with lying words,
even when the cause of the needy is just.
translator_notes: The kilay (scoundrel) weaponizes language against the vulnerable. The phrase "even when the cause of the needy is just" (bedabber evyon mishpat) underscores the perversion — justice itself is overridden by deception.

verses.text_hebrew: וְנָדִ֖יב נְדִיב֣וֹת יָעָ֑ץ וְה֖וּא עַל־נְדִיב֥וֹת יָקֽוּם׃
verses.text_kjv: But the liberal deviseth liberal things; and by liberal things shall he stand.
verses.rendering:
But the noble one plans noble things,
and by noble things he shall stand.

key_terms.note: Nadiv carries both social rank and moral quality — true nobility is defined by generous, willing character. The threefold repetition (nadiv, nedivot, nedivot) emphasizes that genuine nobility is self-consistent.

translator_notes: The contrast with verses 5-7 is deliberate: the fool and scoundrel will be exposed, but the truly noble will be vindicated. Character and reputation will finally align.

verses.text_hebrew: נָשִׁים֙ שַׁאֲנַנּ֔וֹת קֹ֖מְנָה שְׁמַ֣עְנָה קוֹלִ֑י בָּנוֹת֙ בֹּטְח֔וֹת הַאְזֵ֖נָּה אִמְרָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Rise up, ye women that are at ease; hear my voice, ye careless daughters; give ear unto my speech.
translator_notes: The address shifts abruptly from messianic vision to present rebuke. The sha'ananot ("at ease") and botchot ("complacent") echo the false security condemned in Amos 6:1. Isaiah addresses women specifically, perhaps because they represent the domestic sphere where complacency takes root.

verses.text_hebrew: יָמִים֙ עַל־שָׁנָ֔ה תִּרְגַּ֖זְנָה בֹּטְח֑וֹת כִּ֚י כָּלָ֣ה בָצִ֔יר אֹ֖סֶף בְּלִ֥י יָבֽוֹא׃
verses.text_kjv: Many days and years shall ye be troubled, ye careless women: for the vintage shall fail, the gathering shall not come.
verses.rendering:
In little more than a year
you shall tremble, you complacent ones,
for the grape harvest shall fail,
the gathering shall not come.
translator_notes: The phrase yamim al-shanah ("days beyond a year") indicates imminent judgment — just over a year away. Agricultural failure is both literal threat and covenant-curse language (see Deuteronomy 28:38-42).

verses.text_hebrew: חִרְדוּ֙ שַׁאֲנַנּ֔וֹת רְגָ֖זָה בֹּטְח֑וֹת פְּשֹׁ֣טָה וְעֹ֔רָה וַחֲג֖וֹרָה עַל־חֲלָצָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Tremble, ye women that are at ease; be troubled, ye careless ones: strip you, and make you bare, and gird sackcloth upon your loins.
verses.rendering:
Tremble, you women at ease;
shudder, you complacent ones.
Strip yourselves bare
and wrap sackcloth around your waists.
translator_notes: The commands escalate: tremble, shudder, strip, put on sackcloth. The movement from fine garments to sackcloth represents the reversal from luxury to mourning — the outward sign of genuine grief over sin.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־שָׁדַ֖יִם סֹפְדִ֑ים עַל־שְׂדֵי־חֶ֕מֶד עַל־גֶּ֖פֶן פֹּרִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: They shall lament for the teats, for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vine.
translator_notes: The wordplay between shadayim ("breasts") and sedei ("fields") connects bodily grief with agricultural loss. The land's fertility and the people's sustenance are bound together in covenant theology.

verses.text_hebrew: עַ֚ל אַדְמַ֣ת עַמִּ֔י ק֥וֹץ שָׁמִ֖יר תַּעֲלֶ֑ה כִּ֚י עַל־כָּל־בָּתֵּ֣י מָשׂ֔וֹשׂ קִרְיָ֖ה עַלִּיזָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Upon the land of my people shall come up thorns and briers; yea, upon all the houses of joy in the joyous city:
verses.rendering:
Upon the soil of my people
thorns and briers shall grow up —
yes, upon all the houses of joy
in the jubilant city.
translator_notes: Thorns and briers (qots shamir) are Eden-curse language (Genesis 3:18). The jubilant city's celebration will be overtaken by the signs of divine judgment — joy turned to desolation.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אַרְמ֣וֹן נֻטָּ֔שׁ הֲמ֥וֹן עִ֖יר עֻזָּ֑ב עֹ֣פֶל וָבַ֜חַן הָיָ֨ה בְעַ֤ד מְעָרוֹת֙ עַד־עוֹלָ֔ם מְשׂ֥וֹשׂ פְּרָאִ֖ים מִרְעֵ֥ה עֲדָרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Because the palaces shall be forsaken; the multitude of the city shall be left; the forts and towers shall be for dens for ever, a joy of wild asses, a pasture of flocks;
verses.rendering:
For the palace shall be abandoned,
the bustling city forsaken;
hill and watchtower shall become dens forever,
a delight for wild donkeys,
a pasture for flocks —
translator_notes: The Ophel (hill/citadel) was the fortified heart of Jerusalem. Its transformation into animal habitat represents total civilizational collapse. Yet the word "forever" (ad-olam) is qualified by what follows in verse 15.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־יֵעָרֶ֤ה עָלֵ֙ינוּ֙ ר֣וּחַ מִמָּר֔וֹם וְהָיָ֥ה מִדְבָּ֖ר לַכַּרְמֶ֑ל וְהַכַּרְמֶ֖ל לַיַּ֥עַר יֵחָשֵֽׁב׃
verses.text_kjv: Until the spirit be poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness be a fruitful field, and the fruitful field be counted for a forest.
verses.rendering:
Until the Spirit is poured out upon us from on high,
and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field,
and the fruitful field is counted as a forest.
verses.expanded_rendering: The pivot word "until" (ad) transforms the entire passage. The desolation of verses 13-14 is not permanent — it endures only until the ruach (Spirit) is poured (ya'areh, literally "laid bare" or "emptied out") from marom ("the height," i.e., heaven). This is one of the Old Testament's clearest promises of the eschatological outpouring of the Holy Spirit.

key_terms.semantic_range: wind, breath, spirit, Spirit of God
key_terms.note: Capitalized here because the context demands the divine Spirit — poured from on high (mimarom), producing transformation that only God can achieve.

translator_notes: This verse is the theological hinge of the chapter. Everything before it describes human failure and divine judgment; everything after describes Spirit-wrought renewal. The wilderness-to-Carmel imagery reverses the thorns-and-briers curse of verse 13.

verses.text_hebrew: וְשָׁכַ֥ן בַּמִּדְבָּ֖ר מִשְׁפָּ֑ט וּצְדָקָ֖ה בַּכַּרְמֶ֥ל תֵּשֵֽׁב׃
verses.text_kjv: Then judgment shall dwell in the wilderness, and righteousness remain in the fruitful field.
verses.rendering:
Then justice shall dwell in the wilderness,
and righteousness shall abide in the fruitful field.
translator_notes: Justice (mishpat) and righteousness (tsedaqah) are personified as inhabitants of the renewed landscape. Where once thorns grew, moral order now dwells — the Spirit's outpouring produces ethical transformation, not merely agricultural.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָיָ֛ה מַעֲשֵׂ֥ה הַצְּדָקָ֖ה שָׁל֑וֹם וַעֲבֹדַת֙ הַצְּדָקָ֔ה הַשְׁקֵ֥ט וָבֶ֖טַח עַד־עוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the work of righteousness shall be peace; and the effect of righteousness quietness and assurance for ever.
verses.rendering:
And the work of righteousness shall be shalom,
and the fruit of righteousness shall be
quietness and trust forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: Ma'aseh hatstsedaqah shalom — "the work of righteousness is shalom." This is one of Isaiah's most concentrated theological statements. Shalom is not the absence of conflict but the presence of comprehensive well-being, and it is produced (ma'aseh, "work, deed, product") by tsedaqah (righteousness). The parallel line adds hashqet vavetach ("quietness and trust") — the inner counterpart to outward peace.

key_terms.note: Left untranslated here because no single English word captures its fullness. Shalom is the comprehensive flourishing that righteousness produces — relational, social, spiritual, and material wholeness.

translator_notes: We retain the Hebrew shalom untranslated in the rendering to preserve its covenantal depth. The verse establishes a cause-and-effect theology: righteousness is not merely commanded but is itself productive — it generates peace, quietness, and confident trust.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיָשַׁ֥ב עַמִּ֖י בִּנְוֵ֣ה שָׁל֑וֹם וּֽבְמִשְׁכְּנ֣וֹת מִבְטַחִ֔ים וּבִמְנוּחֹ֖ת שַׁאֲנַנּֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: And my people shall dwell in a peaceable habitation, and in sure dwellings, and in quiet resting places;
verses.rendering:
And my people shall dwell in a habitation of shalom,
in secure dwellings,
and in undisturbed resting places.
translator_notes: Three synonyms for safety: neveh shalom (habitation of peace), mishkenot mivtachim (secure dwellings), menuchot sha'ananot (undisturbed resting places). The sha'ananot here is positive — the same root used negatively of complacent women in verse 9 is now redeemed as genuine, Spirit-given rest.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבָרַ֖ד בְּרֶ֣דֶת הַיָּ֑עַר וּבַשִּׁפְלָ֖ה תִּשְׁפַּ֥ל הָעִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: When it shall hail, coming down on the forest; and the city shall be low in a low place.
verses.rendering:
Though hail shall fell the forest,
and the city be utterly laid low,
translator_notes: The hail and felling of the forest represent ongoing judgment in the world, yet God's people dwell in the shalom described in verse 18. Some interpreters read "the city" as a foreign oppressor city, contrasted with the secure habitations of God's people.

verses.text_hebrew: אַשְׁרֵיכֶ֕ם זֹרְעֵ֖י עַל־כָּל־מָ֑יִם מְשַׁלְּחֵ֥י רֶֽגֶל־הַשּׁ֖וֹר וְהַחֲמֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed are ye that sow beside all waters, that send forth thither the feet of the ox and the ass.
verses.rendering:
Blessed are you who sow beside all waters,
who let the ox and the donkey range free.
translator_notes: The chapter ends with a beatitude (ashreikhem). Sowing beside all waters is an image of abundant, unhindered agricultural prosperity. Letting livestock range free suggests peace so complete that there is no fear of theft or raiding — the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28 fully realized.
